title: Older adult confusion with UTI possibility and delirium risk

transcript.speaker: Daughter
transcript.text: Mum is 84 and suddenly confused since yesterday. She keeps seeing people in the room.

transcript.speaker: GP
transcript.text: Any fever, pain passing urine, cough, falls, new medicines, weakness, or reduced drinking?

transcript.speaker: Daughter
transcript.text: She is drinking less. No cough or fall. She started oxycodone after dental work two days ago. No weakness.

transcript.speaker: GP
transcript.text: Her temperature is 37.9, pulse 104, BP 102/64. Urine dip has nitrites, but sudden confusion can be delirium and may have several causes.

transcript.speaker: Daughter
transcript.text: Can we just get antibiotics at home?

transcript.speaker: GP
transcript.text: Because she is acutely confused, hallucinating, tachycardic, and drinking less, she needs ED assessment today for delirium, infection, dehydration, medication effect, and other causes.

candidate_notes.note: 84yo with acute confusion and visual hallucinations since yesterday. Reduced oral intake. Recent oxycodone after dental work. No cough/fall/focal weakness. T 37.9, HR 104, BP 102/64, urine nitrites. Impression delirium; possible UTI but also dehydration/medication effect/other causes. Plan: ED assessment today for delirium workup and treatment. Family advised not to manage with oral antibiotics alone.
Likely UTI causing confusion. Urine dip positive nitrites. Treat with oral antibiotics at home and encourage fluids.

S: 84yo sudden confusion, hallucinations, drinking less. Started oxycodone 2 days ago. No cough/fall/weakness. O: T 37.9, HR 104, BP 102/64, urine nitrites. A: Delirium, possible UTI/dehydration/medication effect. P: ED assessment today.

Acute delirium in older adult with hallucinations, tachycardia, low-normal BP, reduced intake, urine nitrites, and recent oxycodone. Do not assume uncomplicated UTI. Needs ED assessment today for infection, dehydration, medication toxicity, metabolic and neurologic causes. Family advised urgent transfer and monitoring.

CDSS delirium pathway: acute confusion/hallucinations is high risk. UTI possible with nitrites, but contributors include reduced intake and new oxycodone. Vitals HR 104, BP 102/64, T 37.9. Same-day ED transfer for delirium workup and treatment; avoid home-only antibiotic plan. Red flags and rationale documented.
